Based on the search results, I found several resources about reverse engineering Claude Code internals and architecture:

## Reid Barber's Analysis

Reid Barber has documented Claude Code's architecture, describing its tools which are functions that the model can invoke, including examples like the BashTool for command execution and FileReadTool/FileWriteTool/FileEditTool for filesystem operations.

The architecture separates the text-based user interface, core logic, external service interactions, tool implementations, and state management, and it supports extending functionality through external tools via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Claude Code uses a REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop), allowing users to enter prompts in natural language or use specific slash commands for predefined actions, and the application processes this input and either handles it directly for local operations, or constructs and sends queries to the backend language model.

## Additional Reverse Engineering Resources

The search also revealed several other reverse engineering projects and analyses:

- A log parsing tool called parser.js and visualization tool called visualize.html are available to analyze Claude Code's LLM interactions by opening a recorded log file to view the entire conversation.

- A comprehensive analysis of over 50,000 lines of obfuscated source code for Claude Code v1.0.33 documents four core technical findings, including real-time steering mechanisms, hierarchical multi-agent architecture, intelligent context management, and enhanced security.

Reid Barber's blog at reidbarber.com contains detailed technical breakdowns of Claude Code's internals and architecture.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Claude Code Agent Teams token cost and real-world usage experience in 2026:

## Token Cost Overview

A 3-teammate team uses roughly 3-4x the tokens of a single session doing the same work sequentially, but the time savings on complex tasks more than justify the cost. Agent teams use significantly more tokens than a single session. Each teammate has its own context window, and token usage scales with the number of active teammates.

## When Agent Teams Make Sense

For research, review, and new feature work, the extra tokens are usually worthwhile. For routine tasks, a single session is more cost-effective. They work best when teammates can operate independently on distinct scopes while still benefiting from communication.

## Real-World Performance

In practice, teammates typically spawn within 20-30 seconds and begin producing results within the first minute.

## Pricing for Claude Code Teams

Claude Code Teams costs $150/month per developer with a minimum of 5 seats. That means the minimum monthly cost is $750/month.

## Practical Challenges

Its experimental status, however, brings challenges such as unpredictable behavior and high token usage, making careful planning essential for effective use.

The general consensus from 2026 usage reports is that while Agent Teams consume more tokens, the parallel execution and time savings justify the cost for complex, multi-faceted tasks—particularly for code review, debugging, and feature development scenarios.

Links: [{"title":"From Tasks to Swarms: Agent Teams in Claude Code | alexop.dev","url":"https://alexop.dev/posts/from-tasks-to-swarms-agent-teams-in-claude-code/"},{"title":"alexop.dev","url":"https://alexop.dev/"}]

I found the article you're looking for! Here's a technical analysis of the key concepts from "From Tasks to Swarms: Agent Teams in Claude Code":

## Architecture Overview

Agent teams are a new experimental feature in Claude Code where multiple sessions coordinate as a team—with a shared task list, direct messaging between teammates, and a team lead that orchestrates the whole thing.

## Key Technical Components

**Session Structure:**
Each teammate is a full Claude Code session with its own context window, loading the same project context (CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, skills) but not inheriting the lead's conversation history. Task files on disk and SendMessage are the only coordination channels—there's no shared memory.

**Team Lead Role:**
The team lead observes, coordinates, enforces quality, and synthesizes results.

**Task Coordination:**
Tasks move through three states, with teammates self-claiming or getting assigned by the lead, executing in waves based on dependency chains, and file locking preventing double-claiming.

## Trade-offs

Solo sessions give you full control but limited throughput, while agent teams give you maximum compute but you're trusting the AI to coordinate itself.

The article was published on February 8, 2026 on alexop.dev and discusses how this experimental feature enables parallel work distribution similar to real engineering teams.

Based on my search results, here's an overview of the paddo.dev article "Agent Teams: The Switch Got Flipped":

## Summary

Anthropic launched TeammateTool as "agent teams" alongside the Opus 4.6 release. You can enable it with one environment variable: CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1.

## Background Discovery

Two weeks prior, kieranklaassen discovered TeammateTool by running strings on Claude Code's binary, finding a fully-implemented multi-agent orchestration system. That post became the most-shared piece on the blog.

## Key Features

Agent teams feature peer-to-peer messaging (not hub-and-spoke), delegate mode that restricts the lead to coordination-only, competing hypothesis debugging where agents argue with each other, and file-locked task claiming to prevent race conditions.

The architecture uses a team lead + teammates design where one session coordinates while others work independently.

## Architecture and Use Cases

Anthropic's Nicholas Carlini built a production C compiler using 16 parallel Claude agents over two weeks, producing 100,000 lines of Rust that compiles Linux 6.9 on x86, ARM, and RISC-V with 99% pass rate on GCC torture tests, and compiles QEMU, FFmpeg, SQLite, PostgreSQL, Redis, and Doom.

## Known Limitations

Current rough spots include no session resumption, one team per session, no nested teams, and split panes don't work in VS Code.

Links: [{"title":"Sixteen AI Agents Built a C Compiler From Scratch - Slashdot","url":"https://developers.slashdot.REDACTED"},{"title":"Claude Opus 4.6 spends $20K trying to write a C compiler • The Register","url":"https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/09/claude_opus_46_compiler/"},{"title":"Sixteen Claude Agents Built a C Compiler without Human Intervention... Almost - InfoQ","url":"https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/02/claude-built-c-compiler/"},{"title":"Building a C compiler with a team of parallel Claudes","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-c-compiler"},{"title":"16 AI Agents Just Built a C Compiler From Scratch.","url":"https://medium.com/@REDACTED"},{"title":"No Humans, Just 16 Claude AI Agents Built a Fully Functional C Compiler, Shocking Developers - Gizmochina","url":"https://www.gizmochina.com/2026/02/07/no-humans-just-16-claude-ai-agents-built-a-fully-functional-c-compiler-shocking-developers/"},{"title":"Claude Agents Just Built a Fully Functioning C Compiler With Zero Human Input","url":"https://www.analyticsvidhya.com/blog/2026/02/claude-agents-built-c-compiler/"},{"title":"16 Claude Code Agents Built a New C Compiler for $20,000 From Scratch in 2 weeks – Matthew Griffin | Keynote Speaker & Master Futurist","url":"https://www.fanaticalfuturist.com/2026/01/16-claude-code-agents-built-a-new-c-compiler-for-20000-from-scratch-in-2-weeks/"},{"title":"ISP.Tools | Claude AI Agents Build New C Compiler","url":"https://www.isp.tools/claude-ai-agents-build-new-c-compiler/"},{"title":"Anthropic's new AI model can create C compiler from scratch","url":"https://www.newsbytesapp.com/news/science/anthropic-new-claude-ai-model-can-build-c-compilers/story"}]

Here are the detailed findings about Nicholas Carlini's C compiler project with 16 Claude agents:

## Project Overview

Anthropic researcher Nicholas Carlini set 16 instances of Claude Opus 4.6 loose on a shared codebase over two weeks to build a C compiler from scratch, producing a 100,000-line Rust-based compiler capable of building a bootable Linux 6.9 kernel on x86, ARM and RISC-V architectures.

## Technical Implementation

Each instance operated inside its own Docker container, independently claiming tasks via lock files and pushing completed code to a shared Git repository. No orchestration agent directed traffic.

## Costs and Scale

The project ran through nearly 2,000 Claude Code sessions and cost about $20,000 in API fees.

## Results and Capabilities

The compiler achieved a 99% pass rate on the GCC torture test suite and can compile major open source projects including PostgreSQL, SQLite, Redis, FFmpeg and Doom. But it lacks a 16-bit x86 backend and calls out to GCC for that step, its assembler and linker remain buggy, and it produces less efficient code than GCC running with all optimizations disabled.

## Key Challenges and Solutions

Carlini invested significant effort building test harnesses and feedback systems to keep the agents productive, and the model hit a practical ceiling at around 100,000 lines as bug fixes and new features frequently broke existing functionality. When agents got stuck on the Linux kernel, Carlini employed GCC as a compiler oracle: each agent used GCC to compile a random subset of the kernel tree while Claude's compiler handled the remainder, refining its output only on that subset.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Large Language Models Miss the Multi-Agent Mark":

## Overview

This paper examines Multi-Agent Systems of Large Language Models (MAS LLMs), noting that much of this literature appropriates the terminology of MAS without engaging with its foundational principles.

## Key Findings

The paper highlights critical discrepancies between MAS theory and current MAS LLMs implementations, focusing on four key areas: the social aspect of agency, environment design, coordination and communication protocols, and measuring emergent behaviours.

### Main Criticisms

The authors argue that many MAS LLMs lack multi-agent characteristics such as autonomy, social interaction, and structured environments, and often rely on oversimplified, LLM-centric architectures.

**Social Interaction Deficits:** LLMs are trained in isolation to respond to users' requests, rather than to interact with each other, leading to poor performance and unexpected failures when LLMs are benchmarked to identify other agents' beliefs, desires and intentions.

**Coordination Issues:** The AutoGen framework is cited as a prominent example of shortcomings in coordination and communication, specifically the lack of native asynchronicity, with the paper criticizing its implementation of asynchronous operations as an exception rather than the norm.

**Environment Design:** LLMs struggle with maintaining a consistent representation of the environment due to hallucinations and limited memory, and environments are often textual with issues including limited context length and ambiguity.

The paper advocates for better integration of established multi-agent systems theory into LLM-based implementations.

I found the BrightCoding reverse engineering report on Claude Code. Here's what the search results reveal:

## Key Findings from the BrightCoding Report

The ShareAI Lab completed a full-stack reverse engineering study of Anthropic's Claude Code v1.0.33, de-obfuscating over 50,000 lines of obfuscated JavaScript/TypeScript into 102 coherent chunks analyzed with a hybrid static, dynamic, and LLM-assisted pipeline.

### Core Architecture Discoveries

The report revealed several key technical components:

1. **Steering Bus**: A zero-latency "Steering" bus (h2A) is a dual-buffer async queue sustaining over 10,000 messages per second without back-pressure.

2. **Multi-Agent Runtime**: A tiered multi-agent runtime with no master loop, I2A sub-agents, and task agents with sandboxed privilege isolation.

3. **Context Management**: A 92% threshold context compressor (wU2 module) that prunes chat history while preserving critical tokens.

4. **Security**: A 6-layer security gate where every tool invocation passes six orthogonal checks, from UI keystroke to syscall.

### Research Approach

The study was conducted as a purely academic exercise with no DRM circumvention or redistribution of proprietary code, aiming for reproducible science with all tooling and intermediate artifacts released.

The report provides detailed system architecture diagrams and technical documentation covering the entire stack from user interfaces through the agent orchestration layer to storage and persistence mechanisms.

Pan1∗ Shuyi Yang2∗","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.13657"},{"title":"Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.13657v1"},{"title":"Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? - ADS","url":"https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025arXiv250313657C/abstract"},{"title":"Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?, Mert Cemri+, arXiv'25 · Issue #1816 · AkihikoWatanabe/paper_notes","url":"https://github.com/AkihikoWatanabe/paper_notes/issues/1816"},{"title":"ICLR Why Do Multiagent Systems Fail?","url":"https://iclr.cc/virtual/2025/33314"},{"title":"[2503.13657v1] Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657v1"},{"title":"GitHub - multi-agent-systems-failure-taxonomy/MAST","url":"https://github.com/multi-agent-systems-failure-taxonomy/MAST"},{"title":"A System-Level Taxonomy for Reliable AI Applications","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.19933"},{"title":"Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? Anonymous Author(s) Affiliation Address","url":"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=MqBzKkb8eK"}]

I found the paper you're looking for! Here's what the research on multi-agent LLM system failures reveals:

## Paper Overview

Despite enthusiasm for Multi-Agent LLM Systems (MAS), their performance gains on popular benchmarks are often minimal, highlighting a critical need for a principled understanding of why MAS fail.

## The Taxonomy (MAST)

Researchers developed the Multi-Agent System Failure Taxonomy (MAST) to systematically classify these failures. The study introduces MAST-Data, a comprehensive dataset of 1600+ annotated traces collected across 7 popular MAS frameworks.

The taxonomy was developed through rigorous analysis of 150 traces, guided closely by expert human annotators and validated by high inter-annotator agreement (kappa = 0.88), identifying 14 unique modes, clustered into 3 categories: (i) system design issues, (ii) inter-agent misalignment, and (iii) task verification.

## Key Finding

The identified failures require more complex solutions, highlighting a clear roadmap for future research.

The paper demonstrates that failures occur across different models (GPT4, Claude 3, Qwen2.5, CodeLlama) and tasks (coding, math, general agent), demonstrating improvement headrooms from better MAS design.

You can access the full paper at arXiv:2503.13657, and the researchers have open-sourced their dataset and tools on GitHub.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about your query. Here's what the search revealed:

## Claude Code Swarm Orchestration Skill by Kieran Klaassen

Kieran Klaassen created a "Claude Code Swarm Orchestration Skill" - a complete guide to multi-agent coordination using Claude Code's TeammateTool and Task system. The resource is available as a GitHub Gist at gist.github.com/kieranklaassen/4f2aba89594a4aea4ad64d753984b2ea.

## TeammateTool Operations

TeammateTool features 13 core operations supporting enterprise-grade agent orchestration. The operations include team management functions like spawnTeam, discoverTeams, requestJoin, approveJoin, and rejectJoin.

## Key Architecture

The system enables multi-agent coordination through a team structure where a Leader coordinates with Teammates via messaging inbox, with teammates able to message each other, and a task list that tracks completed, in-progress, and pending tasks with ownership and blocking relationships.

The gist documents multiple interaction patterns including Leader, Swarm, Pipeline, Council, and Watchdog patterns.

## Implementation Details

The unlocked capabilities include delegation mode for spawning specialist teams, shared task boards with dependencies, parallel teammate execution, and inter-agent messaging via TeammateTool. The documentation is based on Claude Code v2.1.19 and was tested and verified on 2026-01-25.

Based on the search results from charlesjones.dev and related sources, here's a comprehensive overview of when Agent Teams beat Subagents:

## Key Performance Differences

The deciding question is whether your workers need to communicate with each other. Use subagents when you need quick, focused workers that report back. Use Agent Teams when teammates need to share findings, challenge each other, and coordinate on their own.

## When Agent Teams Win

Subagents have one fundamental limitation: they can only report back to the parent. They can't talk to each other. Agent teams remove that limitation.

Agent team members message directly, challenge each other's findings, and self-coordinate.

## Specific Use Cases Where Agent Teams Outperform

Agent Teams excel in parallel code reviews with different focus areas (security, performance, testing), debugging with competing hypotheses where agents argue theories, and multi-module feature development where each teammate owns a different layer.

## When to Stick with Subagents

For sequential tasks, same-file edits, or work with tight dependencies, a single session or subagent patterns are more cost-effective. If your workers don't need to communicate with each other, the overhead of an Agent Team isn't worth it.

## Cost Consideration

A 5-person team uses roughly 5x the tokens of a single session. It's only worth it for tasks where parallel exploration adds real value—code reviews, debugging, multi-module features.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about Terminal-Bench 2.0 agentic coding evaluation benchmark results for 2026:

## Benchmark Overview

Terminal-Bench 2.0 is a benchmark composed of 89 tasks in computer terminal environments inspired by problems from real workflows, with each task featuring a unique environment, human-written solution, and comprehensive tests for verification.

## Performance Results

Frontier models and agents resolve less than 65% of tasks, with smaller models scoring around 15%.

Some specific model results include:

- OpenAI's Codex, powered by the gpt-5-codex model, has a verified score of 42.8%.
- DeepAgents CLI (powered by Sonnet 4.5) scored approximately 42.5% on Terminal Bench.

## Key Improvements in Terminal-Bench 2.0

Terminal-Bench 2.0 includes 89 carefully curated tasks and is substantially more challenging than its predecessor, with tasks better representing frontier challenges that distinguish truly capable agents.

One significant improvement is the dramatic increase in task quality and reproducibility, with Terminal-Bench 2.0 eliminating problematic tasks, and every task now being reproducible, properly specified, and genuinely solvable.

The benchmark covers diverse scenarios from training machine learning models, to building and running Linux from its source code, to reverse engineering binary files.

data.prompt:
I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

content.content:
# Claude Code's Hidden TeammateTool System

## Discovery Method
The system was found through binary analysis: `strings ~/.local/share/claude/versions/2.1.29 | grep TeammateTool` revealed "a fully-implemented multi-agent orchestration system hiding inside Claude Code's binary."

## The 13 Operations

TeammateTool comprises three operational categories:

**Team Lifecycle Management:**
- `spawnTeam` / `discoverTeams` / `cleanup`
- `requestJoin` / `approveJoin` / `rejectJoin`

**Coordination & Communication:**
- `write` (direct messaging)
- `broadcast` (all-team messaging)
- `approvePlan` / `rejectPlan`

**Graceful Shutdown:**
- `requestShutdown` / `approveShutdown` / `rejectShutdown`

## Directory Structure
The system uses organized file storage:
```
~/.claude/
├── teams/{team-name}/
│   ├── config.json
│   └── messages/{session-id}/
└── tasks/{team-name}/
```

## Environment Variables
Operational configuration relies on:
- `CLAUDE_CODE_TEAM_NAME`
- `CLAUDE_CODE_AGENT_ID`
- `CLAUDE_CODE_AGENT_TYPE`

## Access Control
Two functions gate the feature: `I9()` and `qFB()` must both return true. In public releases, they don't.

## Architectural Patterns
The discovered system mirrors established multi-agent approaches including Leader (hierarchical direction), Swarm (parallel processing), Pipeline (sequential workflows), Council (multi-perspective decisions), and Watchdog (quality monitoring).

content.content:
# Claude Code Agent Teams: Technical Architecture Summary

## Core Coordination Mechanisms

**Team Structure**: One lead agent spawns multiple independent teammates, each running as a separate Claude Code instance with its own context window. The lead coordinates through a shared task list with dependency tracking and an inbox-based messaging system.

**Communication Protocol**: Unlike subagents that "report back to main agent only," teammates communicate directly with each other. The architecture supports "direct messaging between agents - not just reporting back to lead."

## Task Management

Tasks operate in three states: pending, in-process, and completed. Dependencies block unresolved tasks; "auto-unblocking happens when a blocking task finishes." File locking prevents race conditions during task claiming.

Storage: `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json` holds metadata; `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/` stores task lists.

## Messaging & Control

**Display Modes**:
- In-process (default): All teammates run in main terminal; use `Shift+Up/Down` to select teammates
- Split panes: Via tmux/iTerm2 showing all output simultaneously

**Delegate Mode**: Press `Shift+Tab` to restrict leads to coordination-only tools, preventing distraction from implementation work.

**Plan Approval**: Teammates work read-only until leads approve approaches, useful for "risky work."

## Performance Context

The document emphasizes that "LLMs perform worse as context expands." Single agents degrade at ~60% completion on complex multi-service tasks. Multi-agent patterns formalize specialization: "each agent a narrow scope and clean context" yields better reasoning within domains.

**Token Cost Trade-off**: "Each teammate is a separate Claude instance"—agent teams consume significantly more tokens than single sessions but justify the cost through parallelization on properly scoped work.

## When Teams Outperform Single Agents

Effective scenarios include:
- "Competing hypotheses for debugging" with adversarial debate
- "Parallel code review with different lenses" (security, performance, tests)
- Cross-layer feature work (frontend/backend/tests in parallel)
- Research with parallel exploration

Sequential tasks, same-file edits, and heavily dependent work favor single sessions.

## Known Limitations

- No session resumption for in-process teammates after `/resume`
- "One team per session, no nested teams"—prevents runaway costs and maintains human oversight
- Split panes require tmux/iTerm2 (not VS Code, Windows Terminal)
- Permissions propagate from lead to all teammates
- Shutdown can be slow as teammates finish current requests

content.content:
# Technical Details: Claude C Compiler Project

## Agent Configuration
- **Number of agents**: 16 parallel Claude instances
- **Model**: Opus 4.6
- **Duration**: Nearly 2,000 Claude Code sessions over two weeks
- **Cost**: Just under $20,000 in API expenses

## Coordination Mechanism
The system employs a straightforward synchronization approach:

1. **Lock files**: Each agent claims tasks by creating text files in a `current_tasks/` directory (e.g., `current_tasks/parse_if_statement.txt`)
2. **Git-based conflict resolution**: When two agents attempt the same task, git's synchronization forces reassignment
3. **Workflow**: Agents pull upstream changes, merge modifications, push updates, then remove locks
4. **Conflict handling**: "Merge conflicts are frequent, but Claude is smart enough to figure that out"

## Container Architecture
- Fresh Docker container per agent with mounted repository at `/upstream`
- Each agent clones locally to `/workspace`
- Changes pushed back to upstream after completion
- Enables "infinite agent-generation-loop" with fresh containers

## Compiler Specifications
- **Language**: Rust-based implementation
- **Code size**: 100,000 lines
- **Dependencies**: Rust standard library only
- **Architecture support**: x86, ARM, RISC-V

## Test Results
- 99% pass rate on compiler test suites
- Successfully passes "GCC torture test suite"
- Can compile: Linux 6.9 (bootable), QEMU, FFmpeg, SQLite, PostgreSQL, Redis
- Demonstrates capability: compiles and runs Doom

## GCC Oracle Technique
To overcome parallel agent bottlenecks during Linux kernel compilation:
- Randomly compiled kernel portions using GCC
- Tested only remaining files with Claude's compiler
- Enabled parallel debugging of different file failures
- Applied delta debugging for file pair interactions

## Critical Limitations
1. **Lacks 16-bit x86 code generation** for real mode boot; delegates to GCC
2. **No native assembler/linker**; uses GCC for these final stages
3. **Not production-ready**: cannot compile all projects
4. **Code efficiency**: generates less optimized code "than GCC with all optimizations disabled"
5. **Implementation quality**: reasonable but "nowhere near the quality of what an expert Rust programmer might produce"

## Practical Ceiling Identified
The project "nearly reached the limits of Opus's abilities." New features "frequently broke existing functionality." The researcher attempted (unsuccessfully) to address several limitations, particularly the 16-bit x86 generator, which produced outputs exceeding memory constraints by approximately 2x.

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

content.content:
# Key Findings from "Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?"

## Failure Categories & Taxonomy

The paper introduces MAST (Multi-Agent System Failure Taxonomy), organizing failures into 3 primary categories:
1. System design issues
2. Inter-agent misalignment
3. Task verification

These categories encompass **14 unique failure modes** identified through rigorous analysis of 150 traces.

## Dataset & Validation

- **MAST-Data**: Comprises "1600+ annotated traces collected across 7 popular MAS frameworks"
- **Inter-annotator agreement**: kappa = 0.88 (high reliability)
- LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline developed for scalable annotation

## Models & Tasks Tested

**Models evaluated:**
- GPT-4
- Claude 3
- Qwen 2.5
- CodeLlama

**Task domains:**
- Coding
- Mathematics
- General agent tasks

## Core Insight

The research demonstrates that "performance gains on popular benchmarks are often minimal" despite enthusiasm for multi-agent systems, indicating that addressing these identified failures requires "more sophisticated solutions" beyond current approaches.

The authors publicly released MAST-Data, the taxonomy, and their LLM annotator to advance future multi-agent system research.

I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

data.prompt:
I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

content.content:
- Improved memory usage by trimming agent task message history after tasks complete
- Improved memory usage during long agent sessions by eliminating O(n²) message accumulation in progress updates
- Fixed API 400 errors ("thinking blocks cannot be modified") that occurred in sessions with concurrent agents, caused by interleaved streaming content blocks preventing proper message merging.
- Simplified teammate navigation to use only Shift+Down (with wrapping) instead of both Shift+Up and Shift+Down.
- Added `last_assistant_message` field to Stop and SubagentStop hook inputs, providing the final assistant response text so hooks can access it without parsing transcript files.
- Fixed /resume silently dropping sessions when the first message exceeds 16KB or uses array-format content (anthropics/claude-code#25721)
- Fixed custom agent `model` field in `.claude/agents/*.md` being ignored when spawning team teammates (anthropics/claude-code#26064)
- Fixed `/resume <session-id>` failing to find sessions whose first message exceeds 16KB (anthropics/claude-code#25920)
- Fixed teammate spinners not respecting custom spinnerVerbs from settings (anthropics/claude-code#25748)
- VSCode: Fixed conversation messages appearing dimmed while the AskUserQuestion dialog is open (anthropics/claude-code#26078)
- Fixed background tasks failing in git worktrees due to remote URL resolution reading from worktree-specific gitdir instead of the main repository config (anthropics/claude-code#26065)
- Fixed Agent Teams teammates failing on Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry by propagating API provider environment variables to tmux-spawned processes (anthropics/claude-code#23561)
- Fixed Task tool (backgrounded agents) crashing with a `ReferenceError` on completion (anthropics/claude-code#22087)
- Fixed /resume showing interrupt messages as session titles
- Fixed Agent Teams using wrong model identifier for Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry customers
- Improved model error messages for Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry users with fallback suggestions
- Fixed background task notifications not being delivered in streaming Agent SDK mode
- Fixed a crash when agent teams setting changed between renders
- Fixed agent teammate sessions in tmux to send and receive messages
- Fixed warnings about agent teams not being available on your current plan
- Added `TeammateIdle` and `TaskCompleted` hook events for multi-agent workflows
- Added support for restricting which sub-agents can be spawned via `Task(agent_type)` syntax in agent "tools" frontmatter
- Fixed an issue where submitting a new message while the model was in extended thinking would interrupt the thinking phase
- Improved error messages for API connection failures — now shows specific cause (e.g., ECONNREFUSED, SSL errors) instead of generic "Connection error"
- VSCode: Added git branch and message count to the session picker, with support for searching by branch name
- Added research preview agent teams feature for multi-agent collaboration (token-intensive feature, requires setting CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1)
- Added "Summarize from here" to the message selector, allowing partial conversation summarization.
- Improved PDF and request size error messages to show actual limits (100 pages, 20MB)
- Added token count, tool uses, and duration metrics to Task tool results
- Fixed rate limit message showing incorrect "/upgrade" suggestion for Max 20x users when extra-usage is unavailable
- Improved `TaskStop` tool to display the stopped command/task description in the result line instead of a generic "Task stopped" message
- Fixed status bar duplicating background task indicator when PR status was shown
- Windows: Fixed console windows flashing when spawning child processes
- Fixed task IDs potentially being reused after deletion
- [VSCode] Fixed message action buttons having incorrect background colors
- Added ability to delete tasks via the `TaskUpdate` tool
- Fixed agents sometimes ignoring user messages sent while actively working on a task
- Fixed task list sometimes showing outside the main conversation view
- Improved task list to dynamically adjust visible items based on terminal height
- Added env var `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TASKS`, set to `false` to keep the old system temporarily
- [SDK] Added replay of `queued_command` attachment messages as `SDKUserMessageReplay` events when `replayUserMessages` is enabled
- Added new task management system, including new capabilities like dependency tracking
- Fixed message rendering bug
- Added `showTurnDuration` setting to hide turn duration messages (e.g., "Cooked for 1m 6s")
- Added inline display of agent's final response in task notifications, making it easier to see results without reading the full transcript file
- Improved the `/tasks` dialog to go directly to task details when there's only one background task running
- Changed task notification display to cap at 3 lines with overflow summary when multiple background tasks complete simultaneously
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS` environment variable to disable all background task functionality including auto-backgrounding and the Ctrl+B shortcut
- Fixed mismatch between background task count in status bar and items shown in tasks dialog
- [VSCode] Added clickable destination selector for permission requests, allowing you to choose where settings are saved (this project, all projects, shared with team, or session only)

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

content.content:
# Verdent AI Multi-Agent Architecture on SWE-bench

## Performance Metrics
- **Resolution Rate:** 76.1% on SWE-bench Verified (500 human-filtered instances)
- **Ranking:** Top position, surpassing single large models like Claude Opus 4 and GPT-5 series (~70%+)
- **Publication Date:** February 2026

## Architecture Overview

The system employs "multi-agent parallel execution" rather than a single monolithic model. The core mechanism involves:

**Parallel Agent Analysis**
Multiple agents simultaneously analyze the same GitHub issue using different strategic approaches. The documentation describes the flow as: orchestrator receives issue → deploys multiple agents with distinct methodologies → agents generate solutions → evaluation selects optimal result.

**Key Operational Principles:**
- "Multiple agents analyze the same problem simultaneously. Each agent can approach with different strategies"
- Error recovery through diversity—"Even if one agent fails, another can find the correct path"
- Collective output selection based on test passage

## Comparison with Single-Model Approaches

| Factor | Single Model | Multi-Agent |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| **Failure Mode** | Single perspective constraint; difficult error recovery | Distributed risk; inherent fault tolerance |
| **Context Handling** | Struggles with complex codebases | Parallel decomposition |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Expensive large models required | "Multiple mid-sized models in parallel may deliver better results per dollar" |

## Technical Distinction

The article emphasizes this represents a paradigm shift: "better collaboration structures rather than bigger models can be the key to superior performance," comparing it to engineering team dynamics versus individual contributors.

I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

data.prompt:
Find and analyze the Claude Code source code to understand the Agent Teams implementation.

1. First find the binary: run `which claude` and `readlink $(which claude)` to trace back to the package
2. The package is likely an npm global install. Check:
   - `npm root -g` 
   - `ls $(npm root -g)/@anthropic-ai/` or similar
   - Also check `~/.claude/` for any bundled JS
   - Check `/opt/homebrew/`, `/usr/local/lib/`, etc.

3. Once you find the source files (likely .js or .mjs bundles), search for these specific implementations:
   - Task file format: how tasks are serialized to `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`
   - Team config format: what goes in `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
   - Message passing: how SendMessage delivers messages between agents (file-based? IPC? stdin injection?)
   - Task claiming: the file locking mechanism mentioned in docs
   - Agent spawning: how teammates are spawned (subprocess? new claude CLI invocation?)
   - Idle detection: how the system knows a teammate is idle
   - The tool definitions for TeamCreate, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, SendMessage

4. Search specifically for:
   - grep for "teams/" and "tasks/" in the source
   - grep for "SendMessage" implementation
   - grep for "file.*lock" or "flock" 
   - grep for "teammate" spawning logic
   - grep for task serialization/deserialization

Use grep/rg extensively on the source files. Read relevant sections when you find matches.

content.content:
- **Team config location**: \`~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json\`
The config file contains a \`members\` array with each teammate's:
- \`name\`: Human-readable name (**always use this** for messaging and task assignment)
- \`agentId\`: Unique identifier (for reference only - do not use for communication)
- \`agentType\`: Role/type of the agent
**IMPORTANT**: Always refer to teammates by their NAME (e.g., "team-lead", "researcher", "tester"). Names are used for:

Based on my extensive investigation, let me compile my comprehensive findings from both the actual file system artifacts and the binary source code:

## Claude Code Agent Teams Implementation Analysis

Based on my thorough analysis of the Claude Code binary (version 2.1.47) and actual file system artifacts in `~/.claude/`, here's what I've discovered:

### 1. **Team File Structure**

The system maintains two parallel file hierarchies:

- **Team Config**: `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`
  - Contains a `members` array with each teammate's information
  - Each member has: `name` (human-readable), `agentId` (unique identifier), `agentType` (role)
  
- **Team Inboxes**: `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/inboxes/{agent-name}.json`
  - Array of messages for each teammate
  - Each message contains: `from`, `text` (JSON-serialized message object), `timestamp`, `read` flag

### 2. **Message Format (Observed in Actual Files)**

From `/Users/tau/.claude/teams/b18e107a-fc7a-414d-811c-9466dbdf1c3f/inboxes/`:

```json
[
  {
    "from": "team-lead",
    "text": "{\"type\":\"task_assignment\",\"taskId\":\"2\",\"subject\":\"...\",\"description\":\"...\",\"assignedBy\":\"team-lead\",\"timestamp\":\"...\"}", 
    "timestamp": "2026-02-18T02:37:17.323Z",
    "read": false
  }
]
```

### 3. **Task File Structure**

Tasks are stored as individual JSON files in `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`:

```json
{
  "id": "6",
  "subject": "Map types and interfaces",
  "description": "Detailed description of what needs to be done",
  "activeForm": "Present continuous form shown during execution",
  "status": "pending|in_progress|completed",
  "blocks": [],
  "blockedBy": []
}
```

**Status values**: `pending`, `in_progress`, `completed`, `deleted`

**Fields**:
- `id`: Numeric task identifier (serialized individually per file)
- `subject`: Imperative form title
- `description`: Full task requirements
- `activeForm`: Present continuous verb for spinner display
- `status`: Lifecycle state
- `blocks`: Array of task IDs this task blocks
- `blockedBy`: Array of task IDs blocking this task

### 4. **Task List Metadata Files**

Each task directory contains:
- `.lock` - Empty file for coordinating access (file-based locking mechanism)
- `.highwatermark` - Contains the highest task ID created (for auto-incrementing)

Example: `.highwatermark` contains `"10"` meaning 10 tasks have been created in this team.

### 5. **Message Types (From Binary Analysis)**

The implementation supports these message types in the `text` field (JSON-serialized):
- `task_assignment` - Assign a task to a teammate with fields: `taskId`, `subject`, `description`, `assignedBy`, `timestamp`
- `shutdown_request` - Request a teammate to shut down
- `shutdown_response` - Approve/reject shutdown with approval flag
- `plan_approval_request` - Request approval for a plan
- `plan_approval_response` - Approve/reject a plan
- `idle_notification` - Sent when agent becomes idle
- `permission_request` - Request permission for sensitive operations
- `permission_response` - Respond to permission requests

### 6. **Agent Spawning & Teammate Execution**

From the binary code analysis (`getTeamName`, `getAgentName`, `getAgentId`, `createTeammateContext`):
- Teammates are spawned as separate Claude CLI processes
- They maintain `AsyncLocalStorage` context with: `agentId`, `agentName`, `teamName`, `parentSessionId`, `color`, `planModeRequired`
- Teammates read from their inbox (`~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/inboxes/{agent-name}.json`)
- Communication happens through file-based message delivery, not IPC

### 7. **Task Claiming & Ownership**

From tool documentation in the binary:
- Tasks don't explicitly have an `owner` field in the JSON format
- Instead, task ownership is implicit: teammates claim tasks via `TaskUpdate` with `owner` parameter
- The `.lock` file provides file-based coordination for concurrent task claiming
- Tasks with `blockedBy` restrictions cannot be claimed until dependencies complete

### 8. **Idle Detection**

The system detects teammates as idle through:
- Monitoring task completion with `TeammateIdle` hook events
- `TaskCompleted` hook events signal when background tasks finish
- Teammates send `idle_notification` messages with completion status
- The `read` flag on messages tracks which messages have been consumed

### 9. **Task Dependencies & Blocking**

- Tasks are coordinated via `blocks` and `blockedBy` arrays
- A task cannot start if `blockedBy` is non-empty
- Tasks automatically become available when all blocking tasks complete
- IDs prefer lowest-first ordering for task claiming

### 10. **Settings & Configuration**

From changelog analysis:
- Feature gated by `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1` environment variable
- Supports custom agent models via `.claude/agents/*.md` files
- Propagates API provider environment variables to spawned teammates
- Supports Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry backends

### 11. **Key Implementation Details**

From binary code (`o0A` exports):
- `getTeammateContext()` - Retrieve current teammate context
- `getTeamName()` - Get current team identifier  
- `getAgentName()` - Get teammate's display name
- `getAgentId()` - Get unique agent identifier
- `isTeammate()` - Check if running as a teammate
- `isTeamLead()` - Check if running as team lead
- `waitForTeammatesToBecomeIdle()` - Synchronization primitive for team lead
- `setDynamicTeamContext()` / `getDynamicTeamContext()` - Dynamic context management

### 12. **File-Based Coordination**

The implementation uses **pure file system coordination**:
- No daemon coordination needed between teammates
- Messages delivered by appending to inbox JSON arrays
- Tasks claimed via `.lock` file coordination
- Highwatermark file tracks task ID sequence
- Each teammate reads their own inbox periodically
- Status persisted in individual task JSON files

This is a simple, decentralized file-based system with no complex IPC or database requirements, making it highly portable and suitable for multi-terminal or remote scenarios.

data.prompt:
I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

content.content:
# Claude Code Architecture: Technical Analysis

## Core System Components

The application uses a **REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)** that processes user input as either natural language prompts, slash commands, or bash commands. The "Core Logic" layer orchestrates the flow by determining input type and routing accordingly.

## Tool Definitions

Claude Code provides approximately 15+ tools that the model can invoke:

**File Operations:**
- `FileReadTool`: Reads text/image files with optional line offsets
- `FileWriteTool`: Creates or overwrites entire files
- `FileEditTool`: Replaces unique string occurrences with context requirements
- `NotebookReadTool`/`NotebookEditTool`: Jupyter notebook manipulation

**Search & Navigation:**
- `GlobTool`: Fast pattern-based file discovery (e.g., `**/*.js`)
- `GrepTool`: Content search using regex across directories
- `LSTool`: Directory structure exploration

**Execution & Agents:**
- `BashTool`: Persistent shell session with state management, git/gh support
- `AgentTool`: Launches independent sub-agents for parallel tasks
- `ArchitectTool`: Generates implementation plans without writing code

**Special Tools:**
- `MemoryReadTool`/`MemoryWriteTool`: Persistent memory directory access
- `ThinkTool`: Model reasoning/thought logging (tau-bench inspired)
- `MCPTool`: Generic wrapper for external Model Context Protocol servers
- `StickerRequestTool`: Easter egg for shipping info collection

## Data Flow Architecture

The documented flow shows: User Input → Input Processing → LLM Query Construction → API Request → Response Processing → Tool Execution Cycle → Follow-up Query → Final Display.

Permission checks occur before tool execution with options for temporary or permanent approval.

## Permission System

A hierarchical model controls tool access:
- Initial trust dialog grants baseline read permissions
- Configuration-based pre-approval checking
- Per-action permission dialogs with temporary/permanent options
- Read-only tools require permission only outside project directories

## Services Integration

- **Claude API Service**: Handles Anthropic/Bedrock/Vertex endpoints with retries and cost tracking
- **Statsig Service**: Feature flags and analytics
- **Sentry Service**: Error reporting with context
- **MCP Client Service**: External server connections via stdio or Server-Sent Events
- **OAuth Service**: Anthropic Console authentication
- **Notifier Service**: Desktop notifications (iTerm2, terminal bell)
- **VCR**: Test fixture recording/replay utility

## Context Management

The system gathers conversation history, project information, and code style guidelines when constructing API requests. State management handles configuration (global/project-specific), command history, and cost tracking.

## Notable Implementation Details

No system prompt contents or exact schema definitions were disclosed. The application was initially released with source maps on NPM, allowing this reverse engineering analysis. MCP integration allows extending functionality through external protocol servers with explicit approval requirements.

content.content:
# Claude Code Swarm Orchestration - Technical Documentation

## Core Primitives

The swarm system consists of five main components:

- **Agent**: A Claude instance capable of using tools
- **Team**: A named group with one leader and multiple teammates
- **Teammate**: A persistent agent with inbox, color, and name
- **Task**: Work items with dependencies and status tracking
- **Inbox**: JSON messaging between agents

## TeammateTool Operations

### Team Management

**spawnTeam**: Creates team infrastructure at `~/.claude/teams/{name}/`
```
Teammate({ operation: "spawnTeam", team_name: "feature-auth" })
```

**discoverTeams**: Lists available teams for joining

**requestJoin**: Submit join request with proposed name and capabilities

**approveJoin/rejectJoin**: Leader accepts or denies membership requests

**cleanup**: Removes team directories (only when no active members exist)

### Communication

**write**: Send targeted message to specific teammate
```
Teammate({ operation: "write", target_agent_id: "security-reviewer", value: "message" })
```

**broadcast**: Send message to all teammates (expensive - N messages for N members)

### Lifecycle Management

**requestShutdown**: Leader initiates teammate exit

**approveShutdown/rejectShutdown**: Teammate confirms or declines termination

## Two Spawn Methods

### Method 1: Subagents (Short-lived)
```
Task({ subagent_type: "Explore", description: "Find auth files", prompt: "..." })
```
Returns results synchronously; no team membership required.

### Method 2: Teammates (Persistent)
```
Task({ team_name: "my-project", name: "security-reviewer", 
       subagent_type: "security-sentinel", run_in_background: true })
```
Joins team, accesses shared task list, communicates via inbox.

## Task System

**TaskCreate**: Define work items
```
TaskCreate({ subject: "Review auth", description: "...", activeForm: "Reviewing..." })
```

**TaskUpdate**: Modify status, ownership, and dependencies
```
TaskUpdate({ taskId: "2", status: "completed" })
TaskUpdate({ taskId: "3", addBlockedBy: ["1", "2"] })
```

**Task Dependencies**: Tasks auto-unblock when blocking tasks complete, enabling pipelines.

## Built-in Agent Types

- **Explore**: Read-only codebase search (Haiku model)
- **Bash**: Execute shell commands
- **Plan**: Architecture design (read-only)
- **general-purpose**: Full tooling capabilities
- **claude-code-guide**: Claude Code documentation queries

## Plugin Agents (compound-engineering)

**Review specialists**:
- `security-sentinel` - Vulnerability analysis
- `performance-oracle` - Bottleneck detection
- `architecture-strategist` - Design compliance
- `kieran-rails-reviewer` - Rails best practices

**Research agents**:
- `best-practices-researcher` - External standards
- `framework-docs-researcher` - Documentation gathering
- `git-history-analyzer` - Code archaeology

## Message Formats

Regular messages contain `from`, `text`, `timestamp`, `read` fields.

Structured messages use `type` field for machine-readable semantics:
- `shutdown_request` / `shutdown_approved`
- `task_completed`
- `idle_notification`
- `plan_approval_request`
- `join_request`
- `permission_request`

## Environment Variables (Auto-set)

```
CLAUDE_CODE_TEAM_NAME="my-project"
CLAUDE_CODE_AGENT_ID="worker-1@my-project"
CLAUDE_CODE_AGENT_NAME="worker-1"
CLAUDE_CODE_PLAN_MODE_REQUIRED="false"
```

## Spawn Backends (Auto-detected)

**in-process**: Same Node.js, hidden execution, fastest startup
**tmux**: Separate panes/windows, visible, persistent across exits
**iterm2**: Split panes in iTerm2 (macOS only, requires `it2` CLI)

Detection priority: Inside tmux → iTerm2 with it2 CLI → External tmux → in-process fallback.

## Orchestration Patterns

### 1. Parallel Specialists
Multiple reviewers analyze code simultaneously without blocking:
```
// Spawn security, performance, simplicity reviewers in single message batch
// Each sends findings to leader inbox independently
```

### 2. Pipeline (Sequential)
Tasks depend on prior completion, auto-progressing:
```
Task #1 (Research) → Task #2 (Plan) → Task #3 (Implement) → Task #4 (Test)
```

### 3. Self-Organizing Swarm
Workers poll TaskList, claim pending tasks, process independently:
```
// Workers compete for available tasks
// Natural load-balancing emerges
// No manual assignment needed
```

### 4. Research + Implement
Synchronous research feeds into subsequent agent work.

### 5. Plan Approval Workflow
Teammate with `mode: "plan"` sends plan for leader review before proceeding.

### 6. Coordinated Multi-File Refactoring
Task-based file boundaries with downstream dependencies ensure coordination.

## File Structure

```
~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/
├── config.json           # Members, metadata
└── inboxes/
    ├── team-lead.json    # Leader messages
    └── worker-1.json     # Worker messages

~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/
├── 1.json               # Task state and metadata
├── 2.json
└── 3.json
```

## Error Handling

**Cannot cleanup with active members**: Use `requestShutdown` first
**Team does not exist**: Call `spawnTeam` initially
**Agent not found**: Verify name in `config.json`
**Agent type not found**: Check subagent_type prefix and availability

Crashed teammates auto-timeout after 5 minutes; tasks remain claimable.

## Key Distinctions

| Aspect | Subagent (Task) | Teammate (Task + team_name + name) |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Lifespan | Until complete | Until shutdown |
| Communication | Return value | Inbox messages |
| Task access | None | Shared list |
| Team membership | No | Yes |

---

This skill coordinates multi-agent systems through persistent teams, shared task queues with dependencies, and asynchronous inbox messaging—enabling complex workflows from simple parallel reviews to self-organizing swarms.

content.content:
# Architectural Comparison: Claude Flow V3 vs Claude Code TeammateTool

## Core Similarities (92% Overlap)

Both systems implement multi-agent coordination through nearly identical abstractions:

**Team/Swarm Management**: Claude Flow uses `Swarm`/`SwarmId` while TeammateTool employs `Team`/`team_name`. Both support group creation, member discovery, and lifecycle management with maximum member constraints.

**Topology Types**: Each supports flat, hierarchical, and centralized configurations. Claude Flow's "mesh" topology parallels TeammateTool's flat arrangement.

**Agent/Teammate Roles**: Identical role set across both systems: coordinator, coder, tester, reviewer, researcher, architect, and worker agents.

## Key Differences

**Consensus Mechanisms**: Claude Flow implements four algorithms (Raft, Byzantine, Gossip, Paxos) with configurable thresholds. TeammateTool uses "implicit majority" voting without algorithm specification.

**Message Architecture**: Claude Flow's MessageBus supports four priority levels with TTL and acknowledgment requirements. TeammateTool's mailbox uses simpler file-based queuing without priority granularity.

**Performance Specifications**: Claude Flow targets "1000+ msg/sec, <100ms latency." TeammateTool performance metrics remain undocumented.

**Advanced Features**: Claude Flow includes ReasoningBank learning systems and Flash Attention mechanisms. TeammateTool lacks these neural optimizations.

## Coordination Patterns

**Plan Approval Workflow**: Both implement propose→vote→execute cycles. Claude Flow uses formal `ConsensusProposal` and `ConsensusVote` interfaces, while TeammateTool offers `approvePlan`/`rejectPlan` operations.

**Join/Leave Mechanics**: Claude Flow enables implicit agent registration; TeammateTool requires explicit request-approval workflows for both joining and shutdown operations.

**Spawn Backends**: Both support in-process execution and tmux-based spawning, enabling distributed teammate instances.

## Documentation & Transparency

Claude Flow maintains open Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) and published npm releases. TeammateTool remains undocumented, feature-gated within Claude Code v2.1.19+, suggesting deliberate concealment of implementation details.

I need to reverse-engineer how Claude Code's "Agent Teams" feature works internally by examining files on disk.

Do a thorough exploration of:

1. Find where Claude Code is installed:
   - Run: `which claude` to find the binary
   - Run: `npm list -g 2>/dev/null | grep claude` or check `~/.npm`, `~/.local/share/`, etc.
   - Look for the actual JS source bundle

2. Examine `~/.claude/` thoroughly:
   - `ls -la ~/.claude/`
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/teams/` if it exists
   - `ls -laR ~/.claude/tasks/` if it exists
   - Look for any JSON config files related to teams
   - Read any `config.json`, `settings.json`, or similar files

3. If you find the Claude Code source (likely a JS bundle or node_modules):
   - Search for keywords: "TeamCreate", "TaskCreate", "TaskList", "SendMessage", "teammate", "agent_teams"
   - Search for file path patterns like "teams/" or "tasks/"
   - Look for the task file format - how are tasks serialized?
   - Look for the messaging/mailbox implementation
   - Search for "file lock" or "flock" related to task claiming

4. Look at the system prompt that's injected for agent teams:
   - Search for "TeamCreate", "TeamDelete", "TaskCreate", "TaskUpdate", "SendMessage" tool definitions
   - Look for the system prompt templates that get injected when teams are active

5. Check for any temp files, sockets, or IPC mechanisms:
   - Look in /tmp/ for claude-related files
   - Check for unix sockets or named pipes

Read and document every relevant file you find. I want the actual implementation details, file formats, and protocols.

content.content:
# Verdent SWE-bench Verified Technical Report Summary

## Performance Metrics
- **Pass@1 (single attempt):** 76.1% resolution rate
- **Pass@3 (up to three attempts):** 81.2% resolution rate
- These results achieved "with no leaderboard tuning or test-time scaling"

## Multi-Agent Architecture

Verdent employs a coordinated multi-agent system featuring:

**Core Workflow:** "plan-code-verify development cycle" with parallel agents powered by frontier models

**Key Components:**
- Main coding agent for task execution
- Dedicated code-review subagent for quality assurance
- Support for Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT-5 model interchangeability

The system measures "not just the model in isolation, but rather the entire agent system," emphasizing that scaffolding, prompts, tools, output parsing, and context management drive performance.

## Agent Coordination Mechanisms

1. **Todo List System:** Maintains "an explicit, structured todo list throughout task execution," preventing "model drift" on vague requirements

2. **Verification Loop:** "Treats verification as a first-class stage," executing type checks, static analysis, and tests after edits

3. **Code Review Subagent:** Yields approximately 0.5% pass@3 improvement, inspecting diffs and flagging risks

## Leaderboard Comparison

Verdent outperforms:
- Claude Code (with Claude Sonnet 4.5)
- Codex (with GPT-5)

Cross-provider variance for identical models reaches "as high as 1.2%" in pass@1 scores.

## Additional Insights

- Thinking mode improved pass@1 from 80% to 82% (Verdent) and 76% to 78% (Claude Code)
- Ablation study showed minimal toolkit impact, suggesting benchmark limitations for real-world applicability

content.content:
# Claude Code Agent Teams: Technical Architecture

## Team Lead's Control Layer

The team lead operates as an orchestration abstraction that observes, coordinates, enforces quality gates, and synthesizes results. It can monitor which agents are idle or stuck, break work into atomic tasks, manage dependencies, and reassign work if needed. The lead also enforces plan approval and quality gates before agent changes propagate.

## Task State Machine

Tasks flow through three discrete states:

- **Pending**: Available for claiming
- **In Progress**: Claimed by an agent with ownership tracking
- **Completed**: Work finished, dependent tasks unblock

"File locking prevents two teammates from claiming the same task." Teammates self-claim by calling `TaskUpdate` with status changes, or the lead assigns work explicitly.

## Seven Core Primitives

Agent teams operate through seven tool calls:

1. **TeamCreate** — Initializes team namespace and config file
2. **TaskCreate** — Defines work units as JSON files on disk
3. **TaskUpdate** — Changes task status and ownership
4. **TaskList** — Polls available work
5. **Task (with team_name)** — Spawns a teammate as independent session
6. **SendMessage** — Direct communication between any two agents
7. **TeamDelete** — Cleanup after shutdown

## Coordination Channels

Unlike subagents that only report upward, team members communicate directly. "Teammates self-claim or get assigned by the lead." Task files on disk and the `SendMessage` mechanism serve as the coordination backbone. There's no shared memory—only task state and explicit messages.

## Context Isolation

Each teammate runs as a completely independent Claude Code session with its own context window. They inherit the shared project context (CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, skills) but not the lead's conversation history. "Each teammate is a full context window."

## When to Use Teams vs. Solo Sessions

Three-level hierarchy guides selection:

- **Solo**: Direct control, limited throughput (single context window)
- **Subagents**: Parallel workers reporting back only
- **Teams**: Full coordination needed—"large features (parallel tracks), QA swarms (multiple testing perspectives), competing hypotheses (debate and converge)"

## Cost Analysis

"More agents = more tokens = more cost. Each teammate is a full context window." Solo sessions cost ~200k tokens, three subagents ~440k tokens, three-person teams ~800k tokens. The lead ran Sonnet teammates to optimize cost while maintaining coordination capability.

## Real-World Performance: Blog QA

A five-agent swarm tested 146+ URLs across 83 blog posts in approximately 3 minutes, identifying 10 issues by severity without needing browser automation—just parallel curl requests and HTML parsing.

Links: [{"title":"Building Effective AI Agents","url":"https://resources.anthropic.com/building-effective-ai-agents"},{"title":"Building AI Agents with Anthropic's 6 Composable Patterns","url":"https://aimultiple.com/building-ai-agents"},{"title":"How we built our multi-agent research system","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system"},{"title":"Building Effective AI Agents","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents"},{"title":"Anthropic's multi-agent system overview a must read for CIOs | Constellation Research","url":"https://www.constellationr.com/blog-news/insights/anthropics-multi-agent-system-overview-must-read-cios"},{"title":"Effective harnesses for long-running agents","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents"},{"title":"What’s new in Microsoft Foundry | October and November 2025 | Microsoft Foundry Blog","url":"https://devblogs.microsoft.com/foundry/whats-new-in-microsoft-foundry-oct-nov-2025/"},{"title":"Anthropic: Building a Multi-Agent Research System for Complex Information Tasks - ZenML LLMOps Database","url":"https://www.zenml.io/llmops-database/building-a-multi-agent-research-system-for-complex-information-tasks"},{"title":"How AI Agents Actually Work: July 2025 | by Berto Mill | Medium","url":"https://bertomill.medium.REDACTED"},{"title":"How Anthropic Built a Multi-Agent Research System","url":"https://blog.bytebytego.com/p/how-anthropic-built-a-multi-agent"}]

The web search has returned comprehensive results about Anthropic's work on building effective agents and multi-agent patterns. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Building Effective AI Agents

Anthropic's "Building Effective AI Agents: Architecture Patterns and Implementation Frameworks" shares best practices and guidelines for building production-ready agentic systems. The resource covers single-agent designs, multi-agent orchestration, and agentic workflows, with real examples from companies like Coinbase, Intercom, and Thomson Reuters who are building and deploying these systems at scale.

## Multi-Agent Patterns and Orchestration

Anthropic's composable patterns include prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator workers, and an evaluator-optimizer. Anthropic's Research system uses a multi-agent architecture with an orchestrator-worker pattern, where a lead agent coordinates the process while delegating to specialized subagents that operate in parallel.

## Key Design Principles

Anthropic recommends starting with simple prompts, optimizing them with comprehensive evaluation, and adding multi-step agentic systems only when simpler solutions fall short. Additionally, practical guidance includes how to choose between single-agent, multi-agent, and workflow-based architectures and when to use sequential, parallel, and evaluator-optimizer patterns.

## Production Challenges

Anthropic's system employs an orchestrator-worker architecture achieving 90.2% performance improvement over single-agent systems on internal evaluations. However, the lead agent must decompose queries into subtasks and describe them to subagents, with each subagent needing an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools and sources to use, and clear task boundaries.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
